---
title: |
      Introduction to \ 
      Programming in R Part 2:\
      Reports
author: Levi Dolan, Data Librarian for the Health Sciences
format: 
  #pptx:
  revealjs:
      theme: [moon]
      incremental: true
      scrollable: true
      transition: slide
      chalkboard: true
      view-distance: 15
      mobile-view-distance: 10
      footer: "Advancing Reproducibility with Computational Skills"
editor: visual
---

## What can happen

-   Analysis decisions made in the moment, not recorded

-   Methods written months later from memory

-   File naming is arbitrary

-   No clear link between code, data, and publications

-   **Result: Irreproducible research, forgotten reasoning**[^1]

[^1]: Sandve GK, Nekrutenko A, Taylor J, Hovig E. (2013). Ten simple rules for reproducible computational research. *PLoS Comput Biol* 9:e1003285.

## Integrated Documentation Strategy

-   **Auto-Generated File Names**

-   **Self-Updating README Files**

-   **Code-Publication Integration**

## Automate Filenaming

-   20241205143022 = timestamp

-   LD_ProjectARCS = user and project identifiers

-   summary_stats = analysis type

-   .xlsx = format

-   TOGETHER: 20241205143022_LD_ProjectARCS_summary_stats.xlsx

## What the example functions do: Summary

`summary_result <- run_single_analysis("summary")`

-   Calculates descriptive statistics (mean, median, standard deviation, min, max) for every numeric column in your dataset

-   Output: One row with statistical summaries for each numeric variable

-   Useful for: Getting an overview of your data's central tendencies and spread

## What the example functions do: Filter

`filtered_result <- run_single_analysis("filtered", filter_condition = "column_name > 50")`

-   Applies a condition to keep only certain rows of data, then sorts the results. Example: filter_condition = "age \> 50" keeps only participants over 50

-   Output: Reduced dataset containing only rows that meet your condition

-   Useful for: Subsetting data based on criteria, creating focused datasets

## What the example functions do: Group

`grouped_result <- run_single_analysis("grouped", group_by_col = "category")`

-   Groups data by categories (as defined by your columns.) and calculates summary statistics within each group

-   Output: Summary statistics broken down by your grouping variable

-   Useful for: Comparing groups, identifying patterns across categories

## From Static Documentation to Living Documents

-   You have organized R projects and started good data management habits

-   Now let's start automating documentation

-   Our goal is for documents to update themselves as your data changes

## Literate Programming

What's this?!

-   **Code + Text + Results** presented together rather than separately

-   An iterative approach toward finished projects

-   Builds in flexibility in output formats

-   ::: callout-note
    The slides I'm using were made in the tool you'll learn today!
    :::

## What we just did

-   Started with a traditional README - a static project description

-   Created an automated file inventory - a dynamic record that can grow with your project

## This works in Quarto Presentations!

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| results: asis

library(fs)

# Get the actual structure
tree_output <- capture.output(dir_tree(".", recurse = 2))

# Define comments for specific files/folders
comments <- list(
  "raw_data" = " (original data goes here)",
  "processed_data" = " (clean data goes here)", 
  "scripts" = " (code goes here)",
  "outputs" = " (results and charts go here)",
  "GSE113754_GeneLevel_Raw_data.xlsx" = " (main dataset)",
  "GSE113754_filtered_metadata.csv" = " (sample information)",
  "README.qmd" = " (project documentation)"
)

# Add comments to matching lines
for(item in seq_along(tree_output)) {
  line <- tree_output[item]
  for(name in names(comments)) {
    if(grepl(name, line)) {
      tree_output[item] <- paste0(line, comments[[name]])
      break
    }
  }
}

# Print the result
cat("```\n")
cat(paste(tree_output, collapse = "\n"))
cat("\n```\n")
```

::: notes
DEMO: I can add to my comment "outputs" comment "and Quarto doc" and render and it immediately appears in my slides. Rendering also automatically saves the .qmd file.
:::

## When it's time to write up what you did:

Static script:

```         
# analysis.R
data <- read.csv("data.csv")
summary(data)
mean_x <- mean(data$x)
# Output: 42.5
```

Static document:

["Scholarly_Article_About_My_Research.docx"]{style="font-size: 0.7em;"}

[Methods: We loaded the dataset and calculated summary statistics.]{style="font-size: 0.7em;"}

[Results: The mean of variable X was 42.5.]{style="font-size: 0.7em;"}

## Static Limitations

-   If data changes → Report is wrong

-   If analysis changes → Must remember to update

-   No direct link to how reports were generated

## Dynamic Possibility

Quarto Document: "Scholarly_Article_About_My_Research"

Methods: We loaded the dataset with `#| eval: false {r} nrow(data)` observations. These are summary stastics:`#| eval: false {r} data <- read.csv("data.csv") summary(data)`.

Results: The mean of variable x was `#| eval: false {r} mean_x <- mean(data$x)`.

-   Data/analysis changes → Report updates automatically

-   Complete transparency of methods

## Building Reproducible Documents

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
### **What we just built:** {.column width="45%"}

-   **A dynamic document**

-   **Embedded executable code chunks**

-   **Embedded plot**

-   **Introduced template outputs**
:::

::: {.column width="10%"}
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
### **Why it matters:** {.column width="45%"}

-   Directly connects your data and your document

-   Builds transparency into your findings

-   Minimizes typo risk

-   Gives you flexibility in final output formats
:::
::::::

## Reproducibility in Phases

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
CREATING

PHASE

-   Iterative data management

-   Timestamped files

-   Testing and dead ends

-   Exploratory analysis
:::

::: {.column width="10%"}
```         


───►
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
SHARING PHASE

-   Articulating your process

-   Dynamic documents

-   Data necessary to validate and replicate findings

-   Preserving data in repositories
:::
::::::

## Evaluating Reproducibility

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
AS ETHICAL PRACTICE

-   Accountability in research

-   Transparency serves scientific rigor

-   Levels the scientific playing field

-   Reliable translation to clinical practice
:::

::: {.column width="10%"}
```         
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
AS POTENTIAL HARM

-   Data re-identification

-   Informed consent for secondary use of data

-   Intensification of extractive research

-   Data access prioritized over data quality
:::
::::::

## Extending Reproducibility

![](images/FAIR_CARE.png)

<https://www.gida-global.org/care>

## Why ARCS

-   Learn computational skills while establishing best practices

-   Think about the future as you are forming habits

-   Document and automate iteratively

-   Give your work collaborative potential

    ...so reproducibility becomes how you work, not someone else's checklist.
